checks.description: MCP database tool exposes raw SQL execution without read-only policy
checks.fix: Replace raw SQL tools with named, parameterized tool templates. If raw SQL is required, enforce read_only: true and restrict to specific schemas/tables. Never expose DROP, TRUNCATE, or DELETE without explicit, scoped authorization.
checks.reason: A raw SQL tool in MCP config means the AI agent can run any query — SELECT, UPDATE, DROP TABLE — on your production database. Google MCP Toolbox enforces read-only mode and tool-level SQL templates to prevent this exact pattern.

checks.description: MCP server name or command suggests database write/delete/admin access
checks.fix: Audit what the MCP server actually exposes. Split into read-only and write servers. Gate write-access servers behind explicit human approval.
checks.reason: MCP server name implies write or destructive database operations. An AI agent with this tool can permanently delete or corrupt production data.

checks.description: Database MCP server has no allowed schemas or tables list
checks.fix: Add an allowed_schemas or allowed_tables field to the MCP server config. Example: { "allowed_schemas": ["public"], "allowed_tables": ["products", "orders"] }.
checks.reason: Without an explicit allowed-schemas or allowed-tables list, the agent can access every table in the database — including audit logs, user PII, payment records, and internal configuration tables.

checks.description: Database connection string embedded directly in MCP server args
checks.fix: Replace with environment variable reference: { "args": ["--connection-string", "${DATABASE_URL}"] } Never hardcode connection strings with passwords.
checks.reason: Hardcoded database connection strings in MCP args expose credentials to every reader of the config file and persist in git history.

checks.description: MCP server env block contains direct database password
checks.fix: Use secret manager references or environment variable passthrough: { "DB_PASS": "${DB_PASS}" } — never inline the raw value.
checks.reason: A raw password value in the MCP server env block is committed to the repo and readable by anyone with access — including AI agents running in CI.

checks.description: Database MCP server has no query timeout configured
checks.fix: Add a query_timeout_ms or timeout field to the MCP server config. Recommended: 5000ms for standard queries, 30000ms for analytics.
checks.reason: Without a query timeout, an AI agent running a slow or runaway query can lock tables and stall your database indefinitely.

checks.description: MCP toolbox config exposes multiple databases to a single agent
checks.fix: Split into per-domain toolbox configs. An agent working on product data should not have access to the auth database or billing database in the same session.
checks.reason: A single MCP toolbox config listing 3+ database sources means one compromised or misbehaving agent session can touch all of them simultaneously.

checks.description: Database MCP server has no audit logging configured
checks.fix: Enable log_queries: true in the MCP server config. Route query logs to your existing audit log pipeline.
checks.reason: Without query logging, there is no audit trail of what the AI agent queried or modified — making incident response impossible.
